sections.subject: History

entries.title: About the Course
entries.content:
This course includes the following topic(s): Canadian History: Grades 9-10, World History: Grade 9, Ancient History: Grades 7-9

About Canadian History: Grades 9-10
Read about Canadian history from 1800-1900 through books that immerse students in the time period and allow them to see the world through the eyes of those who lived it. Through engaging narratives, biographies, history spines, primary source documents, oral histories, historical fiction, and books that show different perspectives, students come to see history as more than dates and events and develop relationships with people from the past. Viewing images, looking at maps, and working on one's Book of Centuries are also part of developing this relationship.

About Ancient History: Grades 7-9
Read about ancient history from 100 BC-350 AD through biographies, primary source documents, historical fiction, and history spines. Students will view images, reference maps, and update their Book of Centuries as part of the lessons.

About World History: Grade 9
Reading about the lives of people and events from the time period in many different parts of the world gives students a sense of the growing interconnectedness of the world at this time. A Little Book for New Historians in the third term casts a vision for what it means to think Christianly and historically as a student of history. It raises important questions of complexity, nuance, perspective, empathy, and humility as students enter the HS years.

entries.title: Placement & Combining Tips
entries.content:
World History: Grade 9
All the books are appropriate for any HS student. If a HS student is new to the Alveary, especially consider assigning the third term book, A Little Book for New Historians, no matter their grade.

entries.content:
Permission to print for non-commercial use. See Alveary group use policy to use lessons
in a group context.

LINKS: Click text or scan the QR code in the top corner of the lesson plan pages to view
online resources associated with the lessons.

Responsibility for previewing all links rests with the teacher. All links were checked at the
time of publication; however, websites change frequently and may contain objectionable
content. Please report broken links by contacting us through our website.

entries.title: Term Prep & Teacher Tips
entries.content:
Ancient History: Grades 7-9
The Sand-Reckoner is a well-written and well-researched account of a portion of the life of Archimedes. Math, engineering, duty, justice, stewardship, and courage are some of the themes. The setting is Syracuse in 264 B.C. and as such has a few references to immoral behavior representative of the period as well as some light romantic content that you may want to preview to decide what is best for your particular student(s), especially those on the younger end of the age range. Some may choose to read the story aloud to edit on the fly if needed and to encourage discussion. Some may choose to edit some wording in the text. Others will be fine with the content as is. We encourage all to provide an inviting space for students to ask questions and share thoughts as they process what they are reading in their books. Pages to preview: 14, 36, 40-41, 171, 198, 204, 249.  There is always the option of substituting for either of the books scheduled in Grades 4-6 as well.

blocks.text:
• Take a second to recall what you read previously in the same book or topic.

• If the previous passage is difficult to remember, try looking back at any chapter/section headings or illustrations or reading the first paragraph to jog the memory.

• Read the selection assigned OR read for the number of minutes allocated, leaving time for narration and mapwork. Use whichever guide (i.e., pages or minutes) works best for you. You want to read enough to be interesting, but not so much that you feel overwhelmed.

• If a book is challenging for you as a student, try stopping to narrate to yourself silently for a minute every now and then to make sure you are tracking. You might also want to read aloud to yourself. Or you might want to listen to an audiobook version of the text and read along with it. 

• If a book is an easy read, enjoy the ride! 

• Keep an eye out for quotes to put in your commonplace book or events or characters to put down in your Book of Centuries.

• Process and integrate your reading by narrating either orally or in writing. Some lesson plans will give you a suggestion of how best to narrate. 

• To keep yourself on track, you might want to set a timer, so you stop reading with enough time to narrate.

• After your narration, think about how what you read connects to other subjects, other books, and other things you've read, watched, or seen in the past or present. 

• Share any ideas that are particularly interesting to you with your fellow classmates, teachers, parents, friends, or others. You might even want to do a bit more reading or research on something on your own time in the afternoon or evenings.

These lessons are written directly to the student, but to assist them throughout the year, please read through this section and the lesson plans so you are familiar with the instructions and flow of the lessons. Accountability components are added to facilitate teacher/student discussion and growing independence. 

Those educating at home might pick one or two history lessons to read aloud with their students to keep up the habit of oral narration and foster conversation and relationship, and then simply give students these lesson plans and the rest of the books and check in with them throughout the term for conversation, support, and accountability. 

It is valuable for those in a classroom setting to also have a mix of lessons that students complete themselves silently and others read aloud and narrated together. Classroom settings lend themselves well to additional discussion and sharing at the end of lessons, after everyone has narrated either together or independently in writing.

lessons.title: 30m World History: Grade 9 - Lesson 1
lessons.body:
⍞ Materials: A Little Book for New Historians
➜ INTRO
History is more than just a study of the "past." The purpose of our first term reading of the A Little Book of New Historians is to help you engage with the importance of not just knowing dates and places, but also engaging with, what Robert Tracy McKenzie calls "the flesh and blood on the other side of the evidence."
As you read through Dr. McKenzie's history book, be on the look out for interesting quotes or ideas that stand out to you and be sure to mark whatever pages you find interesting. These are places you can return to and copy down in your book
➜ READ, NARRATE
Ch.1 p.11-18
Narrate silently
In one paragraph share your previous expectations of history as a subject and what the reading has challenged or changed.

lessons.title: 30m Ancient History: Grades 7-9 - Lesson 1
lessons.body:
Meeting Sulla
⍞ Materials: A Short History of Ancient Rome, Historical Atlas of the World

➜ INTRO
This year you are learning about ancient Rome. We are not starting at the beginning with the legend of Romulus and Remus in 753 B.C. We are jumping ahead to 88 BC. The Roman Republic had become a world power. It has marched through the Greek world, razed cities like Carthage and unseated powerful people. As Pascal Hughes says, "the march of Rome appears unstoppable." 
However, what had once been a republic which enabled certain people to vote in their leadership was quickly becoming more of a dictatorship which is where one person assumes absolute power. There were a number of factors that led to Sulla becoming the first dictator in the Roman Republic. He assumed power through political violence, manipulation of land and resources, and military coups. In 88 BC the Republic found itself in danger. 
Sulla's story doesn't start at the beginning of his dictatorship. As you read through Sulla's story, make sure to note any questions that come up from the reading. You can either discuss these questions with someone or write them down.

➜ READ, NARRATE & DISCUSS
Ch.6 p.89-99 - end at "political violence tamed" 

➜ LOCATE
⍞ Map Resource: Historical Atlas of the World, p.14-15. Find Carthage, Rome and any other cities that pop up in your reading to familiarize yourself with the world of Ancient Rome.
lessons.teacherNotes:
➜ STUDENT TIP
Be sure to add some people, events, and drawings of artifacts to your book of centuries. Mark potential items with a Post-it note as you read so you remember to find something when you sit down to work in your book?

• IMPORTANT DATES
Carthage is razed by Scipio Aemilianus in 146 BC
Tiberius Gracchus is elected in 133 BC
Sulla becomes dictator in 82 BC
Sulla resigns as dictator in 80 BC and dies in 78 BC

lessons.title: 30m World History: Grade 9 - Lesson 2
lessons.body:
⍞ Materials: A Little Book for New Historians
➜ INTRO
So why should we care about the past? Why is it important that we know these people, places and ideas that have gone before us? I invite you to think on these two questions for a moment before diving into the reading. 
➜ READ, NARRATE
Ch.2
Narrate silently
Respond to the following question in one paragraph: Why should we care about the past?

COMPOSITION

lessons.title: 30m World History: Grade 9 - Lesson 3
lessons.body:
⍞ Materials: Video
➜ INTRO
Take the time to listen to the interview with Dr. Robert Tracy McKenzie. While you are watching it think on your own questions you may have for Dr. McKenzie. 
➜ LISTEN, NARRATE
Listen to Chapter 1 - What is History? 
Narrate silently.
Listen to Chapter 2 - Why Should we Care About the Past?
Narrate silently.
Write down three questions you may have for a historian? Keep these questions as you read through the rest of the book. If you do find an answer, you can make note of it.

links.text: Why Should We Care About the Past?

lessons.title: 30m Ancient History: Grades 7-9 - Lesson 3
lessons.body:
Spartacus and a Revolt
⍞ Materials: A Short History of Ancient Rome, Historical Atlas of the World

➜ INTRO
Rome is built on slavery. in 73 BC, there were more than a million people enslaved in Rome's Italian lands. As Pascal Hughes says, "Some have been bought from foreign merchants, others taken by military conquest, or enslaved as a punishment for criminality. Then there are the unfortunates who were born into bondage, or who are the victims of kidnap by slavers, or who could see no other option in life than to sell themselves into servitude."
However, revolution came to Rome and it was led by a slave named Spartacus. There are modern day movies that praise the bravery of this man and those who rose up with him against the powers that shackled them. Spartacus' story is fraught with bravery and peril. But it would leave him a legend in the history books.  As you read through Spartacus' story, what sticks out you? Make sure to note any questions that come up from the reading. You can either discuss these questions with someone or write them down.

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
Ch.7 p.110-118 - stop at "Third Servile War" 

• How did Spartacus' actions influence the Roman Empire? 

➜ LOCATE
⍞ Map Resource: Historical Atlas of the World, p.14-15. Locate the various places mentioned in your readings.
lessons.teacherNotes:
• IMPORTANT DATES
Spartacus leads his revolt in 73

lessons.title: 30m World History: Grade 9 - Lesson 4
lessons.body:
⍞ Materials: A Little Book for New Historians
➜ RECAP
What do you remember about your reading last time?
➜ READ & NARRATE
Ch.3
Narrate silently
The following quote is from this week's reading. Dr. Mckenzie writes, "I’m going to say something that may surprise you to hear a history professor say, but I also hope you’ll find it freeing: by themselves, historical facts are not just mind numbing. They’re meaningless. Wrench a historical fact from its context and it instantly becomes insignificant." Respond to this idea in two paragraphs. Do you agree or disagree? Why?

lessons.title: 30m Ancient History: Grades 7-9 - Lesson 5
lessons.body:
Introducing Caesar
⍞ Materials: A Short History of Ancient Rome, Historical Atlas of the World

➜ INTRO
Around the end of the 15th century, William Shakespeare wrote a tragedy titled Julius Caesar. The story of Julius Caesar captured audiences' attention then and it still is prominent in our modern day culture. The blood, betrayal and scandal of the story is what has led to this stories permanence. But as you will read, there is more to this story. Julius Caesar becomes a famous leader for a number of reasons. As you read through the story retold by Pascal Hughes, consider the many reasons Julius Caesar's story might attract so much attention.

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
Ch.8 p.128-137 - stop at "even bigger one"

• Why is Julius Caesar's story so infamous? 

➜ LOCATE
⍞ Map Resource: Historical Atlas of the World, p.14-15. Locate the various places mentioned in your readings.
lessons.teacherNotes:
➜ EXTRA HELPING
∞ Link: If you have any interest in reading Shakespeare's Julius Caesar play, you can find it at the link provided.

lessons.title: 30m Ancient History: Grades 7-9 - Lesson 7
lessons.body:
Introducing Octavian
⍞ Materials: A Short History of Ancient Rome, Historical Atlas of the World

➜ INTRO
With Julius Caesar's death, came the question of who would succeed him? That position would fall upon Octavian. But it would not be easy to assume the position as others would be looking to take his place. Make sure to note any questions that come up from the reading. You can either discuss these questions with someone or write them down.

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
Ch.9 p.154-164 - end at "of the Roman World"

• What was the most interesting thing in this week's reading?

➜ LOCATE
⍞ Map Resource: Historical Atlas of the World, p.14-15. Locate the various places mentioned in your readings.
lessons.teacherNotes:
• IMPORTANT DATES

lessons.title: 30m World History: Grade 9 - Lesson 8
lessons.body:
⍞ Materials: A Little Book for New Historians
➜ INTRO
Have you ever read an autobiography? How about an article or another book based on that autobiography. If the answer is yes, then you have consumed both a primary source and a secondary source. The primary source is a  first-hand experience, and the secondary source is the second-hand experience. There are many different kind of primary and secondary sources out there. A primary source can be a letter, a poem, a picture, a song, or perhaps a journal. What kind of primary sources have you run into recently? 
➜ READ, NARRATE
Ch.6
Narrate silently
Respond to the following question in two paragraphs: Why are primary sources important? What do these sources offer historians?  
➜ EXTRA HELPINGS
Primary vs. Secondary Sources: The Differences Explained | Scribbr
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=CPQ95B95bWE

links.text: Primary vs. Secondary Sources: The Differences Explained | Scribbr

lessons.title: 30m Ancient History: Grades 7-9 - Lesson 9
lessons.body:
Nero
⍞ Materials: A Short History of Ancient Rome, Historical Atlas of the World

➜ INTRO
The Emperor Nero is another famous ruler, but his notoriety comes from the evil that he did as Emperor of Rome. Nero was most known for his murder of anyone who opposed or threatened him. And as Jesus' popularity had increased and his teachings had spread, Nero picked out a new target for his ire: the Christians. Martyrdom under Nero would become infamously horrendous. It is from Nero's time that we have many stories of men and women who died for Christ. 

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
Ch.10 p.174-184 - stop at "this bore Nero."

➜ LOCATE
⍞ Map Resource: Historical Atlas of the World, p.14-15. Locate the various places mentioned in your readings.
lessons.teacherNotes:
• IMPORTANT DATES
The Great Fire of Rome in 64 AD

★ TEACHER TIP
This chapter may have sensitive content. Depictions of martyrdom are essential to understanding the early church's plight, but Nero is known for his horrific treatment of people who he saw as enemies

lessons.title: 30m World History: Grade 9 - Lesson 10
lessons.body:
⍞ Materials: A Little Book for New Historians
➜ RECAP
What do you remember about your reading last time?
➜ READ, NARRATE
Ch.8
Narrate silently
The following quote is from this week's reading. Dr. Mckenzie writes, "This is because your goal is not simply to speak to the living, but to be a blessing to them. Here the metaphor of history as a mirror is instructive. It reminds us that we study the past on its own terms but not for its own sake." Respond to this idea in two paragraphs. Do you agree or disagree? Why?

lessons.title: 30m World History: Grade 9 - Lesson 13
lessons.body:
⍞ Materials: Erebus

➜ INTRO
What happens when nations find themselves with war vessels, but no wars? For 1800s Britain, the answer was to send its ships on exploration missions. The Erebus was just one of the ships that made the transition from military vessel to a vessel used for research and exploration. The captains and crew of the vessel changed over the many years it spent on the seas.
In Erebus, you will read a book about a legendary ship. However, there is another benefit to Michael Palin’s book which makes it a helpful read as well as an enjoyable one. Erebus does not just share the story of the ship itself, but of the world in which this ship sailed. As you read through Erebus, take note of the names being mentioned in each chapter.
I also invite you to adventure with the people on Erebus and on the HMS Terror. Pay attention to the letters and journals that are being mentioned and remember that these were real people who experienced a world very unlike our own. And yet, you’ll also see a world that is very familiar too.

➜ ANNOTATE
Erebus is a non-fiction book which makes it a good candidate for annotation. While you are reading through the text you may encounter an interesting point or main idea that you want to underline. You could put a
question mark next to a statement you find unclear. You may place sticky notes on a page that you want to return to. Experiment with different ways to engage with the information in the book. Some students will record themselves reading or talking about the reading. Others may start a discussion with an acquaintance about the material. Try different ways and see what works and what doesn’t work for you. You can discuss your findings with your teacher.

➜ READ
Ch 1.

➜ NARRATE
Write or discuss the world figures and the events that you’ve read about in chapter one of Erebus. Who has appeared in the chapter? Why are they important? What is the historical setting of the book?

lessons.title: 30m Ancient History: Grades 7-9 - Lesson 13
lessons.body:
Boudica
⍞ Materials: A Short History of Ancient Rome, Historical Atlas of the World

➜ INTRO
Transitions of power did not have a large affect on the growth of the empire. Rome kept invading territories, but these people groups resisted. One such group was led by Boudica who would become famous for her story of rebellion and revenge. Make sure to note any questions that come up from the reading. You can either discuss these questions with someone or write them down.

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
Ch.11 p.194-200 - end at "list of concerns"

• What was the most interesting thing in this weeks reading?

➜ LOCATE
⍞ Map Resource: Historical Atlas of the World, p.14-15. Locate the various places mentioned in your readings.
lessons.teacherNotes:
➜ STUDENT TIP
Be sure to add some people, events, and drawings of artifacts to your book of centuries. Mark potential items with a Post-it note as you read so you remember to find something when you sit down to work in your book?

• IMPORTANT DATES
Boudica is killed after her uprising in 61 AD

★ TEACHER TIP
This chapter may have sensitive content. Boudica's story is full of peril and contains violence towards women.

lessons.title: 30m Ancient History: Grades 7-9 - Lesson 15
lessons.body:
Julia Felix 
⍞ Materials: A Short History of Ancient Rome, Historical Atlas of the World

➜ INTRO
One of the most infamous events in history is the eruption of Mount Vesuvius. There are many scholars and archeologists dedicated to understanding and unearthing the many cities impacted by this eruption. Pompeii is the city most people are familiar with as it was completely buried. Pompeii was located in the Bay of Naples and around 10,000 - 20,000 inhabitants. Make sure to note any questions that come up from the reading. You can either discuss these questions with someone or write them down.

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
Ch.12 p.214-227

• What impact did Mount Vesuvius' eruption have on the empire?

➜ LOCATE
⍞ Map Resource: Historical Atlas of the World, p.14-15. Locate the various places mentioned in your readings.

lessons.title: 30m World History: Grade 9 - Lesson 16
lessons.body:

lessons.title: 30m Ancient History: Grades 7-9 - Lesson 16
lessons.body:
Trajan 
⍞ Materials: A Short History of Ancient Rome, Historical Atlas of the World

➜ INTRO
Who is going to be the most successful emperor of Rome? Pascal Hughes writes, "Ruling from AD 98 to 117, Trajan will become one of the most beloved of all the Roman emperors. A man who conquered new territories, grew the empire as far as anyone would and enriched Rome with awe-inspiring building projects. Indeed, the Senate will welcome future emperors with the wish that they be melior Traiano ('better than Trajan') - evidence that he is to be the benchmark for all who follow." Make sure to note any questions that come up from the reading. You can either discuss these questions with someone or write them down.

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
Ch.13 p.229-239 - end at "words, best emperor"

• What was the most interesting thing in this weeks reading?

➜ LOCATE
⍞ Map Resource: Historical Atlas of the World, p.14-15. Locate the various places mentioned in your readings.
lessons.teacherNotes:
• IMPORTANT DATES
Pliny writes to Trajan for advice about the Christians 112 and 113 AD
Trajan grows the empire to it's biggest size in 117 AD
Hadrian's Wall is built in 122 AD

lessons.title: 30m Ancient History: Grades 7-9 - Lesson 19
lessons.body:
Severus
⍞ Materials: A Short History of Ancient Rome, Historical Atlas of the World

➜ INTRO
Pascal Hughes writes, "Almost fifty years earlier, in the African port city of Leptis Magna (in modern day Libya), a high-ranking welcomes a new baby in 145. The child, Lucius Septimius Severus, reflects Rome's multicultural identity, his mother from an Italian background and his father with Punic, or Carthaginian, heritage. Severus can count consuls among his cousins, while his mother's line are the Fulvia, one of Rome's grand patrician families."

Lucius Septimius Severus would become an emperor of Rome and would be known for creating a military monarchy. As you read more about this emperor, make sure to note any questions that come up from the reading. You can either discuss these questions with someone or write them down.

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
Ch.15 p.261-270 stop at "what comes next?"

lessons.title: 30m Ancient History: Grades 7-9 - Lesson 21
lessons.body:
Zenobia and Decline
⍞ Materials: A Short History of Ancient Rome, Historical Atlas of the World

➜ INTRO
Pascal Hughes writes, "A crowd has gathered in Alexandria, Egypt, one day around AD 271. Standing on a ceremonial platform beneath a radiant sun is a woman of about thirty, gazing out across the throng assembled in her honor. Her name is Zenobia, and she is building a reputation for herself as a force to be reckoned with." Make sure to note any questions that come up from the reading. You can either discuss these questions with someone or write them down.

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
Ch.16 p.279-291 stop at "in the east"

•  Discuss the impact of Zenobia on the Roman Empire. 

➜ LOCATE
⍞ Map Resource: Historical Atlas of the World, p.14-15. Locate the various places mentioned in your readings.
lessons.teacherNotes:
• IMPORTANT DATES
Emperor Aurelian stems the growing power of Zenobia in the East in 272 AD
Constantine becomes the first Christian emperor of Rome in 306 AD
Rome Divides into two empires in 395 AD

lessons.body:
Introducing Helena and Constantine
⍞ Materials: A Short History of Ancient Rome, Historical Atlas of the World

➜ INTRO
What happens when a barmaid meets a lavishly dressed Roman officer named Flavius Constantius? A whirlwind romance of course! It's not long before Helena and Flavius Constantius make things official and Helena bears a child who is named Flavius Valerius Constantinus. The child becomes known as Emperor Constantine, the first Christian Emperor. 
Make sure to note any questions that come up from the reading. You can either discuss these questions with someone or write them down.

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
Ch.17 p.297-310

•Discuss Constantine and Helena's relationship and how it influenced the Roman Empire.

lessons.title: 30m Ancient History: Grades 7-9 - Lesson 25
lessons.body:
The Christian Revolution
⍞ Materials: The Witness of Early Christian Women: Mothers of the Church

➜ INTRO
How revolutionary was Christianity in the Roman Empire? Jesus' teachings challenged a lot of different ideas that had come to be accepted as truths, such as the belief that women were inferior. Mike Aquilina writes, "Males were superior (a man would say) because women were inferior. It was obvious to men. It was so obvious -- so obvious that it hardly needed an argument. Fire is hot, water is wet, and women are inferior to men. This unquestioned and unquestionable truth was the basis of all human society."
So what happens when one of Christianity's most prolific writers challenges this idea in his letters? Think about Galatians 3:28 which reads, "There is neither Jew nor Greek, there is neither slave nor free, there is neither male nor female; for you are all one in Christ Jesus." Paul's words would have registered as an attack on Rome's family values.  
Paul promotes the idea that women have a place in the society, a place in the Bible and a place in the early church. As you read through this book, we encourage you take note of anything that stands out, anything you agree with and anything you disagree with. 

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
Ch.1 p.13-26

•  Discuss what ideas in the reading stood out to you? Why?

➜ LOCATE
⍞ Map Resource: Europe 200 AD Map. Locate the various places mentioned in your readings.

lessons.title: 30m Ancient History: Grades 7-9 - Lesson 26
lessons.body:
Meeting Women in the Early Church
⍞ Materials: The Witness of Early Christian Women: Mothers of the Church, Europe 200 AD Map

➜ INTRO
At the beginning of this chapter Mike Aquilina writes, "Jesus of Nazareth specialized in shocking the sensibilities of the religious establishment of his day. it was easy to do, because the establishment was easily shocked. Heal a blind man on the Sabbath, and you've turned the world upside down. But there was hardly anything more shocking than the way he treated women." In the next chapter you will meet just some of the women in the New Testament who walked alongside Jesus and did ministry along with his disciples. As you read through this book, we encourage you take note of the named women and their stories. 

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
Ch.2 p.27-35 "Jesus of Nazareth"-"...its twelve apostles."

•  What surprised you in this chapter? Do you agree with the reading? Why or why not?

⍞ Map Resource: Europe 200 AD Map. Locate the various places mentioned in your readings.

lessons.body:
Thecla
⍞ Materials: The Witness of Early Christian Women: Mothers of the Church

➜ INTRO
The story of Thecla was recalled in a text known as Acts of Paul and Thecla. We have it many different translations and it recalls the life and conversion of Thecla who later became known as a saint. Her story has been met by much incredulity and questions of credibility. Some of Thecla's story is will be shared with you in the next chapter. As you read through it what kind of questions do you have for Thecla?

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
p.46-59 "The story of Thecla"-"...in the house."

•  What surprised you in this chapter? Do you agree with the reading? Why or why not? 

➜ LOCATE
⍞ Map Resource: Europe 200 AD Map. Locate the various places mentioned in your readings.

lessons.title: 30m Ancient History: Grades 7-9 - Lesson 29
lessons.body:
Perpetua and Felicity
⍞ Materials: The Witness of Early Christian Women: Mothers of the Church

➜ INTRO
Perpetua lived in the time of emperor Septimius Severus. Mike Aquilina wrote that Septimius Severus "wasn't persecuting Christians per se at the moment, but he had made it a capital crime for Roman citizens to become Jews or Christians." Perpetua was a noblewoman and was able to write and read. She converted to Christianity and was sentenced to die with her servant Felicity. Her story is different from other accounts because her story was written by her own hand. As you read through it what kind of questions do you have for Perpetua and Felicity?

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
p.60-67 "Perpetua is almost"-"...had willed it."

•  What surprised you in this chapter? Do you agree with the reading? Why or why not? 

➜ LOCATE
⍞ Map Resource: Europe 200 AD Map. Locate the various places mentioned in your readings.

lessons.body:
The Poets and Thinkers
⍞ Materials: The Witness of Early Christian Women: Mothers of the Church

➜ INTRO
Mike Aquilina writes at the beginning of the chapter, "There were a few pagan women who earned fame as poets or philosophers, but they were very few. We can't say that women were commonly poets and philosophers among the Christians either, but the number seems to have been significantly higher." In the next section you'll learned about St. Macrina, St. Marcella, St. Paula and St. Eustochium, and St. Monica. All of these women were were poets and thinkers. s you read through it what kind of questions do you have for women?

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
p.89-101 "There were a few"-"...to preserve it." 

•  What surprised you in this chapter? Do you agree with the reading? Why or why not?

terms.content:
Ancient History: Grades 7-9
· From A Short History of Ancient Rome
Explain how did the Roman Republic fall?

· From A Short History of Ancient Rome
Which of the emperors you read about had the most impact on Ancient Rome? Explain your answer with three examples of their accomplishments?

Ancient History: Grades 7-9
· From A Short History of Ancient Rome
How did conquered nations react to Rome? Use examples from the reading.


· From A Short History of Ancient Rome
What led to the decline of the Roman empire?

Ancient History: Grades 7-9
· The Witness of Early Christian Women
Choose a woman in the New Testament and explain how they benefited the church and how they maneuvered through a culture that devalued them?



· The Witness of Early Christian Women
Choose women from either the martyrs and heroines or poets and thinkers section and summarize her story.
